g.data-narration: Welcome to Polyalphabetic Ciphers - the solution to frequency analysis attacks.
Monoalphabetic ciphers like Caesar cipher can be broken easily using frequency analysis of letters.
g.text: Monoalphabetic ciphers map each plaintext letter to ONE ciphertext letter.
This preserves the frequency distribution - 'E' always becomes the same letter!
Attackers analyze letter frequencies to break the cipher!

g.data-narration: Polyalphabetic ciphers use multiple Caesar shifts - same plaintext letter encrypts to different ciphertext letters!
g.text: Polyalphabetic ciphers use DIFFERENT substitution alphabets for different positions.
The same letter 'A' can encrypt to different letters based on its position!

g.data-narration: The Vigenère cipher is the most famous polyalphabetic cipher, invented in the 16th century and once called 'unbreakable'.
g.text: Invented by Giovan Battista Bellaso, popularized by Blaise de Vigenère.
Used a keyword to determine which Caesar shift to apply to each letter.
Was considered unbreakable for nearly 300 years!

g.data-narration: The Tabula Recta is a 26x26 grid showing all possible Caesar shifts. We look up the encryption based on plaintext and key letters.
g.text: 26x26 grid: Rows = key letters (Caesar shifts), Columns = plaintext letters
A B C D E F G H I J K L M
A A B C D E F G H I J K L M
B B C D E F G H I J K L M N
C C D E F G H I J K L M N O
Row = shift amount from key letter!

g.data-narration: The Vigenère encryption formula: C_i equals plaintext plus key letter, modulo 26. This is just adding two Caesar shifts!
Let's encrypt 'ATTACK ATDAWN' with keyword 'LEMON'. Each keyword letter shifts the plaintext by a different amount.
To decrypt, reverse the process: subtract the key letter from the ciphertext letter modulo 26 to recover plaintext.
The One-Time Pad is the only cipher proven mathematically unbreakable - when used correctly with a truly random key.
g.text: Theoretically unbreakable cipher, invented in 1917 by Gilbert Vernam.
Uses a key that is: truly random, same length as message, used only once, kept secret.

g.data-narration: For OTP to be secure, four conditions must be met: random key, same length as message, used once, kept secret.
Kasiski examination finds repeated ciphertext sequences to guess keyword length - a powerful cryptanalysis technique.
g.text: Find repeated sequences in ciphertext. The distance between repeats reveals keyword length!

g.data-narration: Once keyword length is known, break ciphertext into groups and apply frequency analysis to each - like cracking Caesar ciphers!
g.text: Breaking Process: Frequency Analysis per Group
Once keyword length m is known: split ciphertext into m groups. Each group is a Caesar cipher!
Apply frequency analysis to each group to find the shift and recover keyword letters.

g.data-narration: Compare all polyalphabetic ciphers: Vigenère is practical but breakable; OTP is unbreakable but impractical due to key management.
Remember these key points: polyalphabetic ciphers use multiple alphabets, Vigenère uses keyword, OTP is unbreakable but needs key management.
For the exam: practice Vigenère encryption/decryption, understand Kasiski examination, remember OTP requirements.
g.text: • Practice Vigenère encryption: C = (P + K) mod 26
